network.overall_strategy: This network targets leucine through ten literature-guided, interconnected prebiotic routes that converge on a small set of hub intermediates: hydrogen cyanide, isovaleraldehyde, leucine aminonitrile, pyruvate, and alpha-ketoisocaproate. The design follows the deep research findings by combining: (i) hydrothermal CO2 reduction and keto-acid generation on iron-sulfur/mineral surfaces; (ii) surface spark-discharge and H2S-rich volcanic production of HCN and branched aldehydes, with Strecker chemistry to aminonitriles; and (iii) biochemical/prebiotic reductive amination and transamination steps that convert alpha-ketoisocaproate into leucine under mild, geochemically plausible conditions. The network explicitly reflects the strongest literature-supported leucine routes: Strecker synthesis from isovaleraldehyde, Ni-catalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoisocaproate, hydrothermal thermodynamic favorability, H2S-enhanced spark production, Bucherer-Bergs access to leucine from alpha-ketoisocaproate, and meteoritic delivery as an external but connected source.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface CO2 hydrogenation on iron-sulfur phases gives reduced C1 products, with formate as an entry point to broader organosynthesis.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction follows iron-sulfur vent chemistry in which greigite/mackinawite analogues catalyze CO2 reduction under alkaline hydrothermal conditions, providing reduced carbon feedstocks for protometabolism; thermodynamic support for amino-acid-forming hydrothermal networks under reducing conditions comes from Amend and Shock (1998), with iron-sulfur catalytic framing from Huber and Wachtershauser (1997, 2003).

reactions.name: Magnetite-mediated Fischer-Tropsch-type formation of formaldehyde and acetaldehyde

conditions.other: subseafloor hydrothermal or vent-wall FT-type synthesis under anoxic reducing conditions

reactions.mechanism: CO2-derived carbon is reduced on magnetite/Fe0 surfaces through Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry to small aldehydes that seed chain growth.
reactions.reasoning: McCollom et al. (1999) support Fischer-Tropsch-type hydrothermal generation of hydrocarbons and oxygenates that can act as aldehyde precursors, while the deep report identifies acetaldehyde and formaldehyde as essential steps in the leucine precursor hierarchy; this reaction is used as the hydrothermal source of the C1-C2 carbonyl pool.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from reduced carbon feedstocks
reactions.mechanism: Sequential reduction and C-C bond formation among C1 carbonyl species on mineral surfaces produce alpha-keto acid scaffolds, represented here by pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: The instructions explicitly include pyruvate synthesis on magnetite/greigite in hydrothermal settings, and the literature report emphasizes proto-metabolic alpha-keto acid networks continuous with modern metabolism. This connection is consistent with Kaur et al. (2024) for keto-acid-centered networks and Barge et al. (2019) for hydrothermal alpha-keto acid to amino acid conversion.

reactions.name: Green-rust analogue reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine

conditions.catalyst: mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide analogue represented by iron mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia condenses with the alpha-keto acid and is reduced on iron-rich mineral surfaces to the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. (2019) experimentally demonstrated pyruvate plus ammonia conversion to alanine at 70C and pH 10 in mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides, establishing the general plausibility of hydrothermal reductive amination of alpha-keto acids. The present network uses hydrothermal iron mineral agents to remain within the required mineral list while preserving the literature-derived chemistry.

reactions.name: Volcanic H2S spark-discharge generation of HCN

conditions.temperature: ambient to warm volcanic plume conditions
conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral surfaces as deposition/photochemical interfaces
conditions.other: electrical discharge in H2S-containing reducing gas mixture; post-discharge dissolution into water

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in sulfur-rich volcanic gases generates cyanide and radical intermediates that feed aqueous Strecker chemistry after quenching.
reactions.reasoning: Parker et al. (2011) showed that leucine and isoleucine were generated specifically in H2S-containing Miller-type spark discharge experiments, while Miller and Van Trump (1981) and Ring et al. (1972) support spark-generated HCN as a key precursor to amino acids via Strecker chemistry. Mineral agents here represent realistic surface deposition and photochemical interfaces required by the schema.

reactions.name: Spark/radical and aldol generation of branched aldehydes from formaldehyde and acetaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation and wet-dry concentration; sulfur-rich radical chemistry; aldol/recombination cascades

reactions.mechanism: Small aldehydes undergo UV/radical-assisted recombination and aldol chain growth to branched C4-C5 aldehydes, including isobutyraldehyde and isovaleraldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies radical recombination in spark discharge and aldol condensation cascades as the principal ways to build leucine's branched carbon skeleton, with Parker et al. (2011b) specifically invoking H2S-derived radicals and Sutton, Pulletikurti, and Krishnamurthy (2025) arguing that formaldehyde aldol chemistry inevitably generates branched frameworks. This is one of the necessary connective reactions because no single paper demonstrates the full continuous assembly to isovaleraldehyde under one exact setup.

reactions.name: Classical Strecker synthesis of leucine aminonitrile from isovaleraldehyde

conditions.other: aqueous evaporitic pool or volcanic runoff; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: The aldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, cyanide adds to form the aminonitrile, yielding leucine aminonitrile as the direct Strecker precursor.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical leucine Strecker route described in the research summary and attributed to Miller and Van Trump (1981), with isotopic/mechanistic support from Chimiak et al. (2022). The specific precursor isovaleraldehyde is explicitly identified in the report as the key branched aldehyde for leucine.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of leucine aminonitrile to leucine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis during geothermal cycling or prolonged standing

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolyzes through amide-like intermediates to the racemic amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of Strecker aminonitriles to amino acids is the final step of the classical Strecker pathway to leucine, as summarized from Miller and Van Trump (1981) and discussed mechanistically by Magrino et al. (2021).

reactions.name: Nickel-catalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoisocaproate to leucine
reactions.mechanism: Ammonia forms an imine/iminium equivalent with the alpha-keto acid and H2-driven reduction on Ni-containing surfaces furnishes leucine with high amino-acid selectivity.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. (2024) directly demonstrated leucine formation from alpha-ketoisocaproate at 22C, pH 8, 1 atm H2 over Ni/SiO2-Al2O3, Pd/C, and ground Aletai meteorite with yields of 6-84%, making this the strongest direct route in the network.

reactions.name: Proto-metabolic chain extension from pyruvate to alpha-ketoisocaproate

conditions.other: mild reducing conditions; network-level keto-acid elongation analogous to ancient metabolic core

reactions.mechanism: Reductive C-C bond formation extends a smaller alpha-keto acid framework toward the branched C6 alpha-keto acid alpha-ketoisocaproate.
reactions.reasoning: The deep report identifies the alpha-keto acid pathway as the most conceptually important route for leucine, placing alpha-ketoisocaproate at the junction between proto-metabolism and amino acid synthesis. Kaur et al. (2024) are cited for H2-dependent reductive aldol chemistry over nickel catalysts producing continuous keto-acid reaction networks resembling the ancient metabolic core; this reaction is therefore included as the necessary network bridge.

reactions.name: Transamination of alpha-ketoisocaproate to leucine using alanine as amino donor

conditions.catalyst: Ni2+, Cu2+, Co2+, or V5+ trace metal ions

reactions.mechanism: Metal-ion-assisted amino transfer from alanine to alpha-ketoisocaproate yields leucine while regenerating pyruvate, creating a connected amino/keto acid cycle.
reactions.reasoning: Varma, Muchowska, and Moran (2021) showed that Cu2+, Co2+, V5+, and Ni2+ catalyze transamination between alpha-keto acids and amino acids at room temperature. This provides a literature-supported alternative conversion of alpha-ketoisocaproate to leucine and creates the required cyclic convergence around pyruvate and alanine.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of alpha-ketoisocaproate to leucine hydantoin

conditions.other: shallow pool chemistry with cyanide- and urea-bearing concentrates

reactions.mechanism: The alpha-keto acid enters a cyanide/urea-derived Bucherer-Bergs sequence to give a hydantoin intermediate corresponding to leucine.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. (2022) demonstrated prebiotically relevant Bucherer-Bergs access from alpha-keto acids through hydantoin intermediates. The deep report specifically flags this as an additional route to leucine-family amino acids beyond Strecker and reductive amination.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of leucine hydantoin to leucine

conditions.other: extended aqueous hydrolysis during geothermal cycling

reactions.mechanism: Hydantoin ring opening and hydrolysis release the corresponding alpha-amino acid, leucine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis is the completion step of the Bucherer-Bergs route from alpha-keto acids to amino acids, consistent with the prebiotic hydantoin network reported by Pulletikurti et al. (2022).

reactions.name: Meteorite-delivered leucine input to surface pools

conditions.catalyst: surface adsorption and preservation on minerals
conditions.other: delivery and concentration in aqueous alteration settings or impact-derived sediments

reactions.mechanism: This node represents exogenous leucine delivery associated with meteoritic parent-body aqueous synthesis and subsequent concentration on mineral surfaces after deposition.
reactions.reasoning: Cronin et al. (1981) confirmed leucine in the Murchison meteorite at ppb-ppm levels, and the report treats meteorites as an unambiguous natural source of prebiotic leucine. This step is partly representational rather than a single in situ Earth-surface reaction, but is included to connect exogenous delivery into the same network and is explicitly grounded in meteoritic evidence.

pathways.name: Classical Strecker route from branched aldehyde
pathways.description: Hydrothermal small-carbonyl production feeds surface branched aldehyde formation, followed by canonical Strecker synthesis and aminonitrile hydrolysis to leucine.

pathways.name: H2S volcanic spark-assisted Strecker route
pathways.description: Sulfur-rich spark discharge supplies HCN while hydrothermal aldehydes are branched at the surface, then Strecker chemistry yields leucine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic keto-acid route
pathways.description: CO2 reduction leads to pyruvate, chain extension forms alpha-ketoisocaproate, and Ni-catalyzed reductive amination yields leucine.

pathways.name: Aldehyde-fed keto-acid route to reductive amination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde supports alpha-ketoisocaproate chain extension from pyruvate, followed by direct reductive amination to leucine.

pathways.name: Alanine-mediated transamination route
pathways.description: Pyruvate first becomes alanine under hydrothermal alkaline conditions, while parallel keto-acid chain extension generates alpha-ketoisocaproate; transamination then forms leucine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs route from alpha-ketoisocaproate
pathways.description: The proto-metabolic keto-acid hub is diverted into hydantoin chemistry and hydrolyzed to leucine.

pathways.name: HCN-coupled Bucherer-Bergs route with spark cyanide input
pathways.description: Volcanic spark discharge provides cyanide to a surface Bucherer-Bergs branch from alpha-ketoisocaproate, producing leucine via hydantoin.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical integrated route
pathways.description: A fully cross-environment pathway in which hydrothermal formaldehyde/acetaldehyde and surface HCN together generate leucine aminonitrile, then biochemical hydrolysis yields leucine.

pathways.name: Branched skeleton via isobutyraldehyde progression to leucine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal C1-C2 carbonyls generate isobutyraldehyde as a branch-point intermediate en route to isovaleraldehyde, then Strecker synthesis gives leucine.

pathways.name: Exogenous meteoritic leucine delivery pathway
pathways.description: Leucine produced in parent-body chemistry is delivered to early Earth and captured/concentrated in surface pools, supplementing endogenous synthesis networks.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the stronger routes. Strecker conversion of isovaleraldehyde to leucine aminonitrile and hydrolysis to leucine are literature-aligned and chemically sound. The main weakness is upstream generation of branched aldehydes: magnetite-mediated C1/C2 formation is plausible, but the specific conversion from formaldehyde/acetaldehyde to isovaleraldehyde is under-specified and likely low-yield/messy under prebiotic conditions.
suggestions: Specify stepwise branching via isobutyraldehyde then chain extension to isovaleraldehyde with clearer mineral/energy inputs.
Add explicit hydrolysis conditions for aminonitrile-to-amino acid conversion.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway aligns well with Parker et al. 2011 and the deep research findings that H2S-rich spark systems are especially important for leucine formation. HCN generation is strong and literature-supported. The weak point remains the lumped production of branched aldehydes from simple carbonyls; the route is directionally consistent with spark/radical chemistry but insufficiently resolved mechanistically.
suggestions: Separate spark-radical production of isobutyraldehyde from subsequent one-carbon homologation to isovaleraldehyde.
Clarify whether rxn_006 is surface photochemical, discharge, or evaporitic aldol chemistry, as these imply different conditions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The final reductive amination of alpha-ketoisocaproate to leucine is the single best-supported reaction in the whole network and closely matches Kaur et al. 2024. However, the route to alpha-ketoisocaproate is weak. Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis is plausible in broad outline, but the one-step proto-metabolic chain extension from pyruvate plus acetaldehyde to alpha-ketoisocaproate is highly speculative and not well supported by the cited literature.
suggestions: Replace rxn_010 with a more explicit alpha-keto acid network reflecting literature on metal-catalyzed keto-acid homologation.
Use literature-grounded intermediates such as alpha-ketoisovalerate before alpha-ketoisocaproate.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pathway has a valid target-forming endpoint but relies too heavily on underspecified carbon-chain construction. The transition from simple aldehydes/pyruvate to alpha-ketoisocaproate is the key unresolved problem in leucine prebiotic chemistry, and this network does not solve it convincingly.
suggestions: Decompose the chain-extension sequence into experimentally motivated substeps.
Ground the alpha-ketoisocaproate precursor sequence more directly in Kaur et al. 2024-style proto-metabolic chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Alanine formation from pyruvate is well supported by Barge et al. 2019, and transamination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids is plausible under prebiotic metal-catalyzed conditions. But as in the keto-acid route, the weak link is the unsupported direct generation of alpha-ketoisocaproate.
suggestions: Add metal-ion catalysis details for transamination to better mirror Varma, Muchowska, and Moran 2021.
Introduce alpha-ketoisovalerate or related intermediates to improve biochemical continuity.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Bucherer-Bergs access from alpha-keto acids via hydantoins is literature-supported in general and gives a plausible alternative to Strecker hydrolysis. However, using urea directly as an input is an oversimplification relative to the canonical Bucherer-Bergs reagent set, and upstream alpha-ketoisocaproate generation remains the dominant weakness.
suggestions: Specify cyanide/carbonate and aqueous conditions more explicitly for the Bucherer-Bergs step.
Clarify prebiotic source of urea or replace with more canonical reagent representation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This integrated HCN-fed Bucherer-Bergs route is conceptually coherent and uses literature-supported HCN generation. Its weakness is cumulative: multiple speculative upstream steps are stacked before a moderately plausible hydantoin route. The pathway is not implausible, but confidence is limited by the chain-assembly gap.
suggestions: Improve the alpha-ketoisocaproate synthesis sequence before invoking Bucherer-Bergs conversion.
Distinguish between spark-generated HCN supply and pool-phase hydantoin formation conditions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the best integrated environmental narrative in the network: hydrothermal reduced-carbon generation, transfer to sulfur-rich surface spark/discharge chemistry, then aqueous Strecker and hydrolysis. The route aligns well with the literature emphasis that no single setting optimally supports the full pathway. The principal remaining issue is still the underdeveloped chemistry of branched aldehyde formation.
suggestions: Provide a more explicit environmental handoff from hydrothermal products to surface pools.
Split rxn_006 into at least two reactions to make the branched skeleton buildup more credible.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Using isobutyraldehyde as a branch-point intermediate is consistent with the literature on branched skeleton construction. But the network compresses too much chemistry into a single reaction that outputs both isobutyraldehyde and isovaleraldehyde from formaldehyde and acetaldehyde. As drawn, the progression is directionally right but chemically underspecified.
suggestions: Make isobutyraldehyde to isovaleraldehyde a distinct step.
Add explicit radical or aldol mechanism details and a mineral/energy basis consistent with the stated environment.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the weakest part of the network. Meteorite delivery of leucine is well supported by Cronin et al. 1981, but the reaction as written is not chemically valid: isovaleraldehyde and HCN do not yield 'meteorite-delivered leucine'. Exogenous delivery should be represented as transport/input, not as a synthetic reaction.
suggestions: Represent meteoritic leucine as an external source node or non-reaction transfer event.
Do not connect exogenous delivery to local precursors unless a real transformation is specified.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong: it has sufficient molecule and reaction count, multiple hubs, multiple convergent points, and good global connectivity. Environmental interconnection is also a clear strength, especially the hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical logic. Several key reactions are well aligned with the literature, particularly H2S-assisted spark generation of HCN/leucine precursors, Strecker formation of leucine aminonitrile from isovaleraldehyde, pyruvate-to-alanine reductive amination, transamination logic, and especially Ni-catalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoisocaproate to leucine. However, the network falls below approval threshold because its weakest point is also the central unsolved problem identified in the literature: assembly of leucine's branched C5-C6 skeleton from simple feedstocks. Reactions rxn_006 and especially rxn_010 are too compressed and insufficiently literature-grounded. The meteoritic route is also improperly encoded as a chemical reaction rather than exogenous delivery. Literature alignment is mixed: the network captures the main experimentally demonstrated leucine-producing systems, but some steps exceed the support of the cited findings and a few claims are more speculative than presented. Overall, this is a good but not excellent network, with a convincing high-level strategy but several chemically weak bridging steps.
